title: Abortion Can, Must, and Shall Be Abolished State-by-State
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman argues that the core split between pro-lifers and abolitionists is whether any human authority (including the Supreme Court) can legalize murder, and contends that states have both the right and the duty to nullify Roe and similar federal pronouncements rather than submit to them.
audiences: Pro-life advocate weighing whether to support immediate state abolition bills
State legislator considering nullification language in pro-life legislation
Constitutional law student evaluating state vs. federal authority arguments
Christian pastor wrestling with the duty to defy unjust civil orders
Political activist comparing single-issue lobbying strategies

questions.question: What does the author identify as the central disagreement between pro-lifers and abolitionists?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate weighing whether to support immediate state abolition bills
questions.answer: Silberman frames the divide as a question of authority: whether the Supreme Court (or any governing body) has the legitimate power to declare murder lawful. Pro-life leaders, in his telling, treat SCOTUS rulings as final and binding, while abolitionists insist no human authority can sanction the killing of innocents.
questions.quote: One of the primary differences between pro-lifers and abolitionists is the disagreement regarding whether the supreme court – or any governing entity – has the authority to make murder legal.

questions.question: How does Joe Pojman characterize the states' obligation to obey Roe v. Wade?
questions.audience: Constitutional law student evaluating state vs. federal authority arguments
questions.answer: Pojman, executive director of Texas Alliance for Life, treats SCOTUS rulings as effectively a law of nature that states cannot resist. Silberman quotes him as saying defiance is as impossible as defying gravity, which the article uses as the paradigm case of pro-life unconditional submission.
questions.quote: We could no sooner ignore SCOTUS than the force of gravity.

questions.question: What did Scott Klusendorf say at a 2016 Christian conference about the federal courts and unborn children?
questions.audience: Christian pastor wrestling with the duty to defy unjust civil orders
questions.answer: Klusendorf described federal court rulings in Roe and Casey as having dictated 'from on high' that unborn children have no right to life. Silberman argues that treating the judiciary as 'on high' contradicts both Scripture and the Constitution.
questions.quote: the federal courts have already decided in Roe vs. Wade, the Casey decision, and others, that no unborn children have the right to life. They’ve already dictated that from on high.

questions.question: What is the abolitionist's claim about the legitimacy of any order that legalizes mass murder?
questions.audience: Political activist comparing single-issue lobbying strategies
questions.answer: Abolitionists hold that such orders are void from the moment they are issued, regardless of who issues them, and that the only acceptable response is open defiance. Silberman appeals to a 'natural right to nullify' grounded in America's founding documents.
questions.quote: Roe, and any other order to allow mass murder like it, is null by its nature. The *only* proper response to an order to allow mass murder is active defiance.

questions.question: How does Bryan Zollinger's position on nullification shift between general state sovereignty and abortion?
questions.audience: State legislator considering nullification language in pro-life legislation
questions.answer: Zollinger championed Idaho's State Sovereignty Responsibility Act, which declared undelegated federal acts void, but reversed himself on abolishing abortion, claiming nullification is unrealistic in practice. Silberman uses Zollinger as the pattern of pro-life politicians who will defy Washington on other issues but never to save preborn children.
questions.quote: It’s pretty well accepted that the supreme court is the final say of what’s constitutional… I don’t see any way to get around that unless we went nullification and we all know that doesn’t happen in reality.

questions.question: What evidence does Silberman offer that nullification already happens in practice?
questions.audience: Constitutional law student evaluating state vs. federal authority arguments
questions.answer: He points to the more than half of states that have decriminalized marijuana in defiance of the federal Controlled Substances Act and Gonzales v. Raich. The example is meant to refute the claim that nullification is a fringe or impossible tactic.
questions.quote: Nullification happens *constantly* in reality. More than half the states have decriminalized marijuana in defiance of the federal Controlled Substances Act and the *Gonzales v. Raich* supreme court decision.

questions.question: How does Greg McCortney's record on marijuana compare to his record on abortion abolition?
questions.audience: Political activist comparing single-issue lobbying strategies
questions.answer: McCortney championed Oklahoma's bill to defy federal marijuana law but, as committee chairman in 2020, refused to grant The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act a hearing. Silberman uses the contrast to charge pro-life politicians with selective courage.
questions.quote: Oklahoma State Senator Greg McCortney was the champion of the bill to defy the feds and legalize marijuana in the state. In 2020, he was the committee chairman who denied The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act a hearing.

questions.question: What did Greg Treat say about nullifiers, and how does that contrast with his prior legislative work?
questions.audience: State legislator considering nullification language in pro-life legislation
questions.answer: Treat had authored a 2016 bill nullifying EPA regulations, yet in a 2019 hearing he denounced nullifiers as the 'villains of history' when the topic was abolishing abortion. Silberman highlights the inconsistency as evidence that pro-life politicians weaponize anti-nullification rhetoric only against abolition.
questions.quote: nullifiers are the villains of history.

questions.question: Why, according to the author, do pro-life politicians embrace submission specifically on abortion?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate weighing whether to support immediate state abolition bills
questions.answer: Silberman blames the pro-life lobby itself, which (unlike the marijuana or Second Amendment lobbies) actively pressures legislators to submit to federal authority even when submission means continued mass killing. The implication is that the movement's own institutions have produced the cowardice.
questions.quote: Pro-life politicians do this, in large part, because while the marijuana lobby, and the second amendment lobby, and many other groups urge state legislators to defy the federal government, the pro-life lobby encourages unconditional submission even when it means the mass murder of image bearers of God.

questions.question: What is the author's final verdict on the moral status of complying with Roe?
questions.audience: Christian pastor wrestling with the duty to defy unjust civil orders
questions.answer: Silberman labels failure to defy Roe and Casey as either cowardice or idolatry, leaving no neutral middle ground. He concludes that abortion will be abolished state-by-state regardless of what SCOTUS does.
questions.quote: Failing to do so is cowardice or idolatry. Abortion can, must, and shall be abolished state-by-state regardless of the supreme court’s position on the matter.

related.title: Crime Against Humanity
related.reason: Silberman's article links directly to this piece as the source for the 'natural right to nullify' grounded in the founding documents.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: The standing Abolitionists Rising explainer on nullification provides the theoretical framework Silberman is operating inside.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Companion piece focused on the Bryan Zollinger committee remark cited in this article.

related.title: Are Nullifications Advocates the Villains of American History?
related.reason: Direct rebuttal to the Greg Treat 'villains of history' line quoted here.

related.title: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
related.reason: Documents the Oklahoma committee maneuver that this article holds up as the contradiction with McCortney's marijuana nullification record.

related.title: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
related.reason: Extends the critique of Abby Johnson that begins with her Facebook post quoted in this piece.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Develops the case against Scott Klusendorf's 'on high' deference to the federal judiciary that Silberman attacks here.
